root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

electromagnetism

Current densities

A current density in $\bbr^3$ is a smooth vector field $\vec{J}$; it has SI units of amperes-per-square-meter, or

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{\text{A}}{\text{m}^{2}} = \frac{\text{C}}{\text{s} \times \text{m}^{2}}. \end{equation}

The current through an oriented embedded surface $S\subset \bbr^3$ is given by the flux integral

\begin{equation}\notag I_S \defeq \int_S \vec{J} \cdot \d \vec{A}. \end{equation}

In particular, suppose that we fix a point $x\in \bbr^3$ and consider a square surface $S(\dev)$ of side length $\dev>0$ centered at $x$ and oriented in the direction of the vector $\vec{J}(x)$. Then the magnitude of the vector $\vec{J}(x)$, denoted $J(x)$, is given by the limit

\begin{equation}\notag J(x) = \lim_{\dev \to 0} \frac{I_{S(\dev)}}{\dev^2}. \end{equation}

Thus, the magnitude of the current density $\vec{J}$ at a point $x\in \bbr^3$ is the flux through an infinitesimal surface centered at $x$ oriented in the direction of the flow.
